user_prompt: do you think this will work? will modern browsers block shims?
agent_reply: Modern browsers generally won’t “block a shim” just because it implements crypto in JS. If `webcrypto-shim.min.js` is served as a normal same-origin script, the browser should run it like any other JS file. Your:
should give `script.js` a stable handle to the shimmed crypto object after the shim replaces/fills `window.crypto`.
The main things that can still break it:
- `webcrypto-shim.min.js` must load before `script.js`. Your `index.html` does that.
- The shim must not require blocked features like `eval`, dynamic import, WASM, or remote dependencies. If it is fully bundled/minified, good.
- `getRandomValues` still needs to exist. Most browsers expose `crypto.getRandomValues` even when `crypto.subtle` is hidden on HTTP, but this is worth testing on the target browser.
- Performance may be rough. PBKDF2 100,000 rounds in JS can be noticeably slower than native WebCrypto.
- It is not the same security story as native WebCrypto. Non-extractable `CryptoKey` protection becomes mostly symbolic if the shim is pure JS, because the key material exists in JS-managed memory.

So: I think it will run, and it’s a reasonable compatibility bridge for your local device admin page. But I would treat shim mode as “better than sending plaintext keys around,” not as equivalent to native WebCrypto’s key isolation.

agent_reply: You were right. I don’t think there’s a good justification for using the network key as the Wi-Fi-session transport encryption key here. It had drifted away from your original “encrypt with session-key” intent, so I changed it.
- Browser derives `network_key` from password only long enough to prove login and derive `session_key`.
- Browser no longer stores `networkHmacKey` or `networkAesKey` in `session_security`.
- `/set_cfg`, `/set_custom_pin`, and `/reset_pin_code` bodies are encrypted with the session key.
- `/set_cfg` now sends the session auth headers too.
- Backend authenticates those headers with `authenticateSessionRequest(...)`, derives `session_key`, and decrypts the request body with that session key.
- PIN reset/update uses the same session-key decrypt path.
- Client-to-server POST nonces now force a separate nonce domain from server-to-client `/get_cfg` responses.
So the corrected documentation line is:

> For security levels >= 1, config transport is session-authenticated and session-encrypted. The network key is used to prove the password-derived credential and derive the per-session key; the session key encrypts `/get_cfg` responses and `/set_cfg`/PIN POST bodies.

user_prompt: You’ve helped me benchmark how fast it takes to decrypt a file on-device, it was slow, like a one hour meeting recording would take maybe 20 minutes to decrypt, but, that was mostly because of the microSD card simultaneous reading and writing being a bottleneck.
I want to implement decrypt while downloading. Hopefully this is faster.

The framework for authentication and encrypted communication is already established in the front-end. We’ve also added a unlock button for files that are `*.rec`, right now clicking on them does nothing. This button is shown and hidden appropriately for the given file name and login state.
So in `lib\WifiManager\WebFileHandlers.cpp` we will add a handler for `/decrypted_download`, registered in `lib\WifiManager\WebServer.cpp`

The front-end will construct a JSON object, with “file_name” as a key to indicate which file is being requested, along with a key “action” indicating “download”. This JSON blob is encrypted with the `sessionAesKey` before being sent to `/decrypted_download`. The CPP side of `/decrypted_download` will decrypt it using `uint8_t session_key[]` , failure to decrypt would indicate not being authenticated
Also handle when “action” is “stream”, then the mime-type is one that indicates that it is a `*.wav` audio file. If the key “action” is missing, then use mime-type being octet stream.

If authenticated and the file exists, then stream it back to the client decrypted chunk by decrypted chunk. The indicated download file name should have a `*.wav` extension. The mime-type should be octet stream

user_prompt: see the previous git commit, did I do anything wrong in the merge resolution?
agent_reply: Yes, I found two likely merge-resolution mistakes.

- High: [WebFileHandlers.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:557) registers `request->onDisconnect([] { endWebUpload(); })`, then [line 586](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:586) registers another `onDisconnect` for `cancelUploadFile(session_id)`. In this ESPAsyncWebServer, `onDisconnect()` is a setter, so the second callback overwrites the first. If an upload disconnects after the file is opened, `g_web_upload_active` can stay true forever, and [guiShouldYield](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.cpp:115) will keep the GUI yielding. Fix by using one combined callback that cancels the upload and ends web upload.

- Medium: [index.html](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/index.html:45) kept `<a class="delete-link" href="/delete_file?file_name=">`, but [script.js](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js:249) treats it like a button: sets `disabled`, assigns `onclick`, and does a `fetch(POST)` without `preventDefault()`. Clicking can still follow the stale empty `href`, potentially navigating away or racing/canceling the POST. Best fix is to restore these to `<button type="button" class="delete-link">`, matching the JS side.

I did not change files; this was review-only.
